title: Stovetop Rice Pudding
subtitle: Short-grain rice simmered in whole milk until thick, finished with vanilla
excerpt: A faster version of rice pudding cooked on the hob rather than in the oven. Short-grain rice simmers gently in whole milk with sugar and vanilla, requiring regular stirring for about 40 minutes, until the mixture is thick and the rice is soft throughout. Ready in under an hour.
sourceNotes: Stovetop rice pudding follows the same principle as the baked version but uses gentle simmering on the hob rather than a slow oven. The technique is common across European and British domestic cooking and gives a creamier, looser result than the baked variety.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Reheat gently with a splash of milk, stirring constantly, as the pudding will thicken on cooling.

content.text: The stovetop method gives a creamier, looser rice pudding than the baked version, and it comes together in 40 minutes rather than two hours. The trade-off is that it needs regular stirring throughout. Leave it unattended and the rice will settle on the bottom of the pan and catch. Stay nearby, check every five minutes, and stir thoroughly each time.

The pudding thickens significantly as it cools. Pull it from the heat when it is just a little thinner than the consistency you want. Short-grain rice is the right variety here: arborio or any short-grain variety releases starch slowly and creates the characteristic creamy texture without going gluey.

Combine {{arborio-rice}} of short-grain rice, {{whole-milk}} of whole milk, and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in a large, heavy-based saucepan. Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat, stirring to dissolve the sugar. Reduce the heat to low.

Cook over a low simmer, stirring thoroughly every 5 minutes, for 35, 40 minutes. The rice should be completely soft and the milk reduced to a thick, creamy consistency that coats the back of the spoon. Check for sticking by dragging the spoon across the bottom of the pan, if it leaves a clear line that fills slowly, the pudding is thickening correctly.
Stir in {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla extract. If using, stir in the butter off the heat for a richer finish. Serve immediately in warmed bowls, with jam or a dusting of cinnamon if you like.

items.symptom: Rice is catching and burning on the bottom
items.cause: Heat is too high, or the pan base is too thin
items.fix: Use a heavy-based pan and keep the heat at a gentle simmer, barely bubbling. Stir more frequently, scraping the bottom and sides with each stir.

items.symptom: Pudding is too thin after 40 minutes
items.cause: Heat was too low, or the pan was too wide so the milk spread and didn't reduce
items.fix: Raise the heat slightly and cook for a further 10 minutes, stirring constantly. A narrower pan will also help concentrate the pudding faster.

items.cause: Heat was too low, or the milk wasn't at a proper simmer
items.fix: Make sure the milk is at a steady, visible simmer throughout. Long-grain rice won't work here, use short-grain (arborio or similar) which softens more readily.

items.symptom: Pudding is too thick and gluey when served
items.cause: Overcooked, or left to cool in the pan without enough liquid
items.fix: Remove from the heat when just slightly thinner than you want, it will set further in the bowl. Stir in a splash of warm milk to loosen if it has thickened too much.

content.text: Stovetop rice pudding sits in the same tradition as the baked version but draws on a wider range of European milk-rice preparations, from the French riz au lait to Scandinavian risgrynsgröt. In the British kitchen it was the faster alternative to the baked pudding, suited to a weeknight rather than a Sunday. The basic combination of rice, milk, and sugar has remained unchanged for centuries and appears in cooking manuals from the eighteenth century onwards.
